infobox.key: [Control](/wiki/Political_make-up_of_local_councils_in_the_United_Kingdom "Political make-up of local councils in the United Kingdom")

infobox.value: ([/nɔːrˈθʌmbərlənd/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*nor-THUM-bər-lənd*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[5]](#cite_note-5) is a [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") in [North East England](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England"), on the [border with Scotland](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_border "Anglo-Scottish border"). It is bordered by the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") to the east, [Tyne and Wear](/wiki/Tyne_and_Wear "Tyne and Wear") and [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham") to the south, [Cumbria](/wiki/Cumbria "Cumbria") to the west, and the [Scottish Borders](/wiki/Scottish_Borders "Scottish Borders") council area to the north. The town of [Blyth](/wiki/Blyth,_Northumberland "Blyth, Northumberland") is the largest settlement. Northumberland is the northernmost county in England.

**Northumberland** ([/nɔːrˈθʌmbərlənd/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*nor-THUM-bər-lənd*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[5]](#cite_note-5) is a [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") in [North East England](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England"), on the [border with Scotland](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_border "Anglo-Scottish border"). It is bordered by the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") to the east, [Tyne and Wear](/wiki/Tyne_and_Wear "Tyne and Wear") and [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham") to the south, [Cumbria](/wiki/Cumbria "Cumbria") to the west, and the [Scottish Borders](/wiki/Scottish_Borders "Scottish Borders") council area to the north. The town of [Blyth](/wiki/Blyth,_Northumberland "Blyth, Northumberland") is the largest settlement. Northumberland is the northernmost county in England.

The county has an area of 5,013 km2 (1,936 sq mi) and had an estimated population of 331,420 in 2024, making it the least-densely populated county in England. The south-east contains the largest towns: [Blyth](/wiki/Blyth,_Northumberland "Blyth, Northumberland"), [Cramlington](/wiki/Cramlington "Cramlington"), [Ashington](/wiki/Ashington "Ashington"), [Bedlington](/wiki/Bedlington "Bedlington"), and [Morpeth](/wiki/Morpeth,_Northumberland "Morpeth, Northumberland"), the last of which is the administrative centre. The remainder of the county is rural, the largest towns being [Berwick-upon-Tweed](/wiki/Berwick-upon-Tweed "Berwick-upon-Tweed") in the far north and [Hexham](/wiki/Hexham "Hexham") in the south-west. For local government purposes Northumberland is a [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England") area. The county [historically](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_England "Historic counties of England") included the parts of Tyne and Wear north of the [River Tyne](/wiki/River_Tyne "River Tyne").

The west of Northumberland contains part of the [Cheviot Hills](/wiki/Cheviot_Hills "Cheviot Hills") and [North Pennines](/wiki/North_Pennines "North Pennines"), while to the east the land becomes flatter before reaching the coast. [The Cheviot](/wiki/The_Cheviot "The Cheviot") (815 m (2,674 ft)), after which the range of hills is named, is the county's highest point. The county contains the source of the River North Tyne and much of the South Tyne; near [Hexham](/wiki/Hexham "Hexham") they combine to form the Tyne, which exits into Tyne and Wear shortly downstream. The other major rivers in Northumberland are, from south to north, the [Blyth](/wiki/River_Blyth,_Northumberland "River Blyth, Northumberland"), [Coquet](/wiki/River_Coquet "River Coquet"), [Aln](/wiki/River_Aln "River Aln"), [Wansbeck](/wiki/River_Wansbeck "River Wansbeck") and [Tweed](/wiki/River_Tweed "River Tweed"), the last of which forms part of the Scottish border. The county contains [Northumberland National Park](/wiki/Northumberland_National_Park "Northumberland National Park") and two [national landscapes](/wiki/National_Landscape "National Landscape"): the [Northumberland Coast](/wiki/Northumberland_Coast_National_Landscape "Northumberland Coast National Landscape") and part of the [North Pennines](/wiki/North_Pennines "North Pennines").

Much of the county's history has been defined by its position on a border. In the Roman era most of the county lay north of [Hadrian's Wall](/wiki/Hadrian%27s_Wall "Hadrian's Wall"), and the region was contested between England and Scotland into the Early Modern era, leading to the construction of many castles, [peel towers](/wiki/Peel_tower "Peel tower") and [bastle houses](/wiki/Bastle_house "Bastle house"), and the early modern fortifications at [Berwick-upon-Tweed](/wiki/Berwick-upon-Tweed "Berwick-upon-Tweed"). Northumberland is also associated with [Celtic Christianity](/wiki/Celtic_Christianity "Celtic Christianity"), particularly the tidal island of [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne"). During the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") the area had significant coal mining, shipbuilding, and armaments industries.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Northumberland](/wiki/History_of_Northumberland "History of Northumberland")

### Name origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Name origin")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle_-_nord_hymbra_land.jpg/250px-Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle_-_nord_hymbra_land.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle_-_nord_hymbra_land.jpg)

An early mention of Northumberland as *norð hẏmbra land* "north of Humber land" in the *Anglo-Saxon Chronicle*

The name of Northumberland is recorded in the [Anglo-Saxon Chronicle](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle "Anglo-Saxon Chronicle") as *norð hẏmbra land*, meaning "the land north of the [Humber](/wiki/Humber "Humber")". The name of the kingdom of *[Northumbria](/wiki/Northumbria "Northumbria")* derives from the Old English *[Norþan-hymbre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Nor%C3%BEanhymbre#Old_English "wikt:Norþanhymbre")* meaning "the people or province north of the Humber",[[6]](#cite_note-6) as opposed to [Southumbria](/wiki/Southumbria "Southumbria"), south of the [Humber Estuary](/wiki/Humber "Humber").

### Before the county

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Before the county")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Long_Crag_summit.jpg/250px-Long_Crag_summit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Long_Crag_summit.jpg)

[Long Crag](/wiki/Long_Crag "Long Crag") summit

The land has long been an English frontier zone, and it is now bordered to the north by [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"). Northumberland has a rich [prehistory](/wiki/Prehistory "Prehistory") with many instances of [rock art](/wiki/Rock_art "Rock art"), [hillforts](/wiki/Hillfort "Hillfort") such as [Yeavering Bell](/wiki/Yeavering_Bell "Yeavering Bell"), and [stone circles](/wiki/Stone_circle "Stone circle") such as [the Goatstones](/wiki/The_Goatstones "The Goatstones") and [Duddo Five Stones](/wiki/Duddo_Five_Stones "Duddo Five Stones"). Most of the area was occupied by the [Brythonic](/wiki/Celtic_Britons "Celtic Britons")-[Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts") [Votadini](/wiki/Votadini "Votadini") people, with another large tribe, the [Brigantes](/wiki/Brigantes "Brigantes"), to the south.

During the [Roman occupation of Britain](/wiki/Roman_occupation_of_Britain "Roman occupation of Britain"), most of the present county lay north of [Hadrian's Wall](/wiki/Hadrian%27s_Wall "Hadrian's Wall"). It was controlled by Rome only for the brief period of its extension of power north to the [Antonine Wall](/wiki/Antonine_Wall "Antonine Wall"). The Roman road [Dere Street](/wiki/Dere_Street "Dere Street") crosses the county from [Corbridge](/wiki/Corbridge "Corbridge") over high moorland west of the [Cheviot Hills](/wiki/Cheviot_Hills "Cheviot Hills") to [Melrose, Scottish Borders](/wiki/Melrose,_Scottish_Borders "Melrose, Scottish Borders") ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Trimontium*). As evidence of its border position through medieval times, Northumberland has more castles than any other county in England,[[7]](#cite_note-7) including prominent ones at [Alnwick](/wiki/Alnwick_Castle "Alnwick Castle"), [Bamburgh](/wiki/Bamburgh_Castle "Bamburgh Castle"), [Dunstanburgh](/wiki/Dunstanburgh_Castle "Dunstanburgh Castle"), [Newcastle](/wiki/The_Castle,_Newcastle "The Castle, Newcastle"), and [Warkworth](/wiki/Warkworth_Castle "Warkworth Castle").

Nearly 2,000-year-old Roman boxing gloves were uncovered at [Vindolanda](/wiki/Vindolanda "Vindolanda") in 2017 by the Vindolanda Trust experts, led by [Andrew Birley](/wiki/Andrew_Birley "Andrew Birley"). Being similar in style and function to the full-hand modern boxing gloves, those found at Vindolanda look like leather bands dating back to 120 AD. It is suggested that, based on their difference from gladiator gloves, the gloves were not used in mortal combat, but rather in a sport for promoting fighting skills. The gloves are currently displayed at Vindolanda's museum.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Present-day Northumberland formed the core of the [Anglian](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)") kingdom of [Bernicia](/wiki/Bernicia "Bernicia") from about 547. It was united with [Deira](/wiki/Deira "Deira") (south of the [River Tees](/wiki/River_Tees "River Tees")) to form the kingdom of [Northumbria](/wiki/Northumbria "Northumbria") in the 7th century. The boundaries of Northumbria under King [Edwin](/wiki/Edwin_of_Northumbria "Edwin of Northumbria") (reigned 616–633) stretched from the [Humber](/wiki/Humber "Humber") in the south to the [Forth](/wiki/River_Forth "River Forth") in the north. Northumberland is often called the "cradle of Christianity" in England because Christianity flourished on [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne")—a tidal island north of [Bamburgh](/wiki/Bamburgh "Bamburgh"), also called Holy Island—after King [Oswald of Northumbria](/wiki/Oswald_of_Northumbria "Oswald of Northumbria") (r. 634–642) invited monks from [Iona](/wiki/Iona "Iona") to come to convert the English. The monastery at Lindisfarne was the centre of production of the [Lindisfarne Gospels](/wiki/Lindisfarne_Gospels "Lindisfarne Gospels") (around 700). It became the home of [St Cuthbert](/wiki/Cuthbert_of_Lindisfarne "Cuthbert of Lindisfarne") (c. 634–687, abbot from c. 665), who is buried in [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral").

The Kingdom of Northumbria fragmented into a series of successor states following the Viking invasion. In the south, Viking settlers established the [Kingdom of York](/wiki/Kingdom_of_York "Kingdom of York") between the Humber and Tees. However, Viking influence petered out at the Tees, with the river serving as the northern boundary of the [Danelaw](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw"). Between the Tyne and Tees rivers, the Community of St. Cuthbert emerged as the successors to the Bishops of Lindisfarne; north of the Tyne, the earls of Bamburgh, who traced their lineage back to the Kings of Northumbria, continued to exercise authority and governance over this northern expanse. York was eventually integrated as a shire into the unified Kingdom of England by the House of Wessex. However, the West Saxon governmental structures were not extended beyond Tees, leaving the Earldom of Bamburgh and the Community of St. Cuthbert as contested buffer states with the emerging Kingdom of Scotland.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)
After the battle of [Nechtansmere](/wiki/Nechtansmere "Nechtansmere"), Northumbrian influence north of the Tweed began to decline as the Picts gradually reclaimed the land previously invaded by the Anglian kingdom. In 1018, its northern part, the region between the [Tweed](/wiki/River_Tweed "River Tweed") and the Forth (including [Lothian](/wiki/Lothian "Lothian"), which includes present-day [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh")), was ceded to the Kingdom of Scotland.

### As a county

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: As a county")]

The [Earldom of Northumberland](/wiki/Earl_of_Northumbria "Earl of Northumbria") was briefly held by the Scottish royal family by marriage, between 1139–1157 and 1215–1217.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Scotland relinquished all claims to the region as part of the [Treaty of York](/wiki/Treaty_of_York "Treaty of York") (1237). The earls of Northumberland once wielded significant power in English affairs because, as powerful and militaristic lords in the [Scottish Marches](/wiki/Scottish_Marches "Scottish Marches"), they had the task of protecting England from Scottish retaliation for English invasions.

Northumberland has a history of revolt and rebellion against the government, as seen in the [Rising of the North](/wiki/Rising_of_the_North "Rising of the North") (1569–1570) against [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I"). These revolts were usually led by the earls of Northumberland, the Percy family. [Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare") makes one of the Percys, the dashing [Harry Hotspur](/wiki/Harry_Hotspur "Harry Hotspur") (1364–1403), the hero of his *[Henry IV, Part 1](/wiki/Henry_IV,_Part_1 "Henry IV, Part 1")*. The Percys were often aided in conflict by other powerful Northern families, such as the [Nevilles](/wiki/House_of_Neville "House of Neville") and the Patchetts. The latter were stripped of all power and titles by the victorious Parliamentarians after the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") of 1642–1651.

After the [Restoration](/wiki/English_Restoration "English Restoration") in 1660, the county was a centre for [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") in England, as well as a focus of [Jacobite](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism") support. Northumberland was long a wild county, where outlaws and [Border Reivers](/wiki/Border_Reivers "Border Reivers") hid from the law. However, the frequent cross-border skirmishes and accompanying local lawlessness largely subsided after the [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns") of Scotland and England under [King James I and VI](/wiki/James_I_of_England "James I of England") in 1603.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Northumberland played a key role in the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") from the 18th century on. Many [coal mines](/wiki/Coal_mining "Coal mining") operated in Northumberland until the [widespread closures](/wiki/UK_miners%27_strike_(1984%E2%80%9385)#Pit_closures_announced "UK miners' strike (1984–85)") in the 1970s and 1980s. [Collieries](/wiki/Collieries "Collieries") operated at [Ashington](/wiki/Ashington "Ashington"), Bedlington, [Blyth](/wiki/Blyth,_Northumberland "Blyth, Northumberland"), Choppington, Netherton, [Ellington](/wiki/Ellington,_Northumberland "Ellington, Northumberland"), and [Pegswood](/wiki/Pegswood "Pegswood"). The region's coalfields fuelled industrial expansion in other areas of Britain, and the need to transport the coal from the collieries to the Tyne led to the development of the first railways. [Shipbuilding](/wiki/Shipbuilding "Shipbuilding") and [armaments](/wiki/Armaments "Armaments") manufacture were other important industries before the deindustrialisation of the 1980s.

Northumberland remains largely rural, and is the least densely populated county in England. In recent years, the county has had considerable growth in tourism. Visitors are attracted both to its scenic beauty and to its historical sites.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/ShepherdPhysical1926Northumberland.png/250px-ShepherdPhysical1926Northumberland.png)](/wiki/File:ShepherdPhysical1926Northumberland.png)

Physical geography of Northumberland and surrounding areas

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/N_NE_England_SRTM.png/250px-N_NE_England_SRTM.png)](/wiki/File:N_NE_England_SRTM.png)

Northumberland has a diverse physical geography. It is low and flat near the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") coast and increasingly mountainous towards the northwest. Being in the far north of England, above 55° [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"), and having many areas of high land, Northumberland is one of the coldest areas of the country. But as the county lies on the east coast, it has relatively low rainfall, with the highest amounts falling on the high land in the west.[[12]](#cite_note-met_rain-12)

About a quarter of the county forms the [Northumberland National Park](/wiki/Northumberland_National_Park "Northumberland National Park"), an area of outstanding landscape that has largely been protected from development and agriculture. The park stretches south from the Scottish border and includes [Hadrian's Wall](/wiki/Hadrian%27s_Wall "Hadrian's Wall"). Most of the park is over 240 metres (790 feet) above sea level. The [Northumberland Coast](/wiki/Northumberland_Coast_National_Landscape "Northumberland Coast National Landscape") is also a designated [Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty](/wiki/Area_of_Outstanding_Natural_Beauty "Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty") (AONB). A small part of the [North Pennines](/wiki/North_Pennines "North Pennines") AONB is also in the county.

There is a variety of nature reserves in Northumberland including [Holy Island](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne") National Nature Reserve and Farne Islands National Nature Reserve. Moreover, 50% of England's [red squirrel](/wiki/Red_squirrel "Red squirrel") population lives in the Kielder Water and Forest Park.

[Natural England](/wiki/Natural_England "Natural England") recognises the following natural regions, or [national character areas](/wiki/National_character_area "National character area"), that lie wholly or partially within Northumberland:[[13]](#cite_note-13)

* [North Northumberland Coastal Plain](/wiki/North_Northumberland_Coastal_Plain "North Northumberland Coastal Plain")
* South East Northumberland Coastal Plain
* [Cheviot Fringe](/wiki/Cheviot_Fringe "Cheviot Fringe")
* [Cheviot Hills](/wiki/Cheviot_Hills "Cheviot Hills")
* [Northumberland Sandstone Hills](/wiki/Northumberland_Sandstone_Hills "Northumberland Sandstone Hills")
* [Mid Northumberland](/wiki/Mid_Northumberland "Mid Northumberland")
* [Tyne Gap](/wiki/Tyne_Gap "Tyne Gap") and [Hadrian's Wall](/wiki/Hadrian%27s_Wall "Hadrian's Wall")
* [Border Moors and Forests](/wiki/Border_Moors_and_Forests "Border Moors and Forests")
* [Tyne and Wear](/wiki/Tyne_and_Wear "Tyne and Wear") Lowlands

### Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Geology")]

Main article: [Geology of Northumberland](/wiki/Geology_of_Northumberland "Geology of Northumberland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Rothbury%2C_Northumberland.jpg/250px-Rothbury%2C_Northumberland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rothbury,_Northumberland.jpg)

[River Coquet](/wiki/River_Coquet "River Coquet")

The [Cheviot Hills](/wiki/Cheviot_Hills "Cheviot Hills"), in the northwest of the county, consist mainly of [resistant](/wiki/Resistance_(geology) "Resistance (geology)") [Devonian](/wiki/Devonian "Devonian") [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") and [andesite](/wiki/Andesite "Andesite") [lava](/wiki/Lava "Lava"). A second area of [igneous](/wiki/Igneous "Igneous") rock underlies the [Whin Sill](/wiki/Whin_Sill "Whin Sill") (on which Hadrian's Wall runs), an intrusion of [Carboniferous](/wiki/Carboniferous "Carboniferous") [dolerite](/wiki/Dolerite "Dolerite"). Both ridges support a rather bare [moorland](/wiki/Moorland "Moorland") landscape. Either side of the Whin Sill the county lies on [Carboniferous Limestone](/wiki/Carboniferous_Limestone "Carboniferous Limestone"), giving some areas of [karst landscape](/wiki/Karst_topography "Karst topography").[[14]](#cite_note-geology-14) Lying off the coast of Northumberland are the [Farne Islands](/wiki/Farne_Islands "Farne Islands"), another dolerite outcrop, famous for their bird life.

The [Northumberland Coalfield](/wiki/Northumberland_Coalfield "Northumberland Coalfield") extends across the southeast corner of the county, from the River Tyne as far north as [Shilbottle](/wiki/Shilbottle "Shilbottle"). There were smaller-scale workings for [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal") within the Tyne Limestone Formation as far north as [Scremerston](/wiki/Scremerston "Scremerston").[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) The term 'sea coal' likely originated from chunks of coal, found washed up on beaches, that wave action had broken from coastal outcroppings.

### Green belt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Green belt")]

Further information: [North East Green Belt](/wiki/North_East_Green_Belt "North East Green Belt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Northumberland_Hadrian%27s_Wall_Aerial_2.jpg/250px-Northumberland_Hadrian%27s_Wall_Aerial_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Northumberland_Hadrian%27s_Wall_Aerial_2.jpg)

[Hadrian's Wall](/wiki/Hadrian%27s_Wall "Hadrian's Wall")

Northumberland's [green belt](/wiki/Green_belt_(United_Kingdom) "Green belt (United Kingdom)") is in the south of the county, surrounding Cramlington and other communities along the county border, to afford a protection from the [Tyneside](/wiki/Tyneside "Tyneside") conurbation. The belt continues west along the border, past [Darras Hall](/wiki/Darras_Hall "Darras Hall"), and on to Hexham, stopping before [Haydon Bridge](/wiki/Haydon_Bridge "Haydon Bridge"). Its border there is shared with the [North Pennines](/wiki/North_Pennines "North Pennines") AONB. There are also some separated belt areas, for example to the east of Morpeth. The green belt was first drawn up in the 1950s.

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Politics")]

### County town

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: County town")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Flying_the_flag_for_Northumberland%2C_Hexham_-_geograph.org.uk_-_6420559.jpg/250px-Flying_the_flag_for_Northumberland%2C_Hexham_-_geograph.org.uk_-_6420559.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flying_the_flag_for_Northumberland,_Hexham_-_geograph.org.uk_-_6420559.jpg)

[Flag of Northumberland](/wiki/Flag_of_Northumberland "Flag of Northumberland") flown in [Hexham](/wiki/Hexham "Hexham"), 2020

The historic [county town](/wiki/County_town "County town") was [Alnwick](/wiki/Alnwick "Alnwick"); [assizes](/wiki/Assizes "Assizes") were mainly held in [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne"), with the [county gaol](/wiki/County_gaol "County gaol") in [Morpeth](/wiki/Morpeth,_Northumberland "Morpeth, Northumberland").[[18]](#cite_note-18) Newcastle became a city in 1400, with [county corporate](/wiki/County_corporate "County corporate") status, with both areas having joint assizes.

From the [county council](/wiki/County_council "County council")'s formation in 1889 until 1981 Newcastle was the [county town](/wiki/County_town "County town"), being briefly the county town of two counties when the city became a part of the [Tyne and Wear](/wiki/Tyne_and_Wear "Tyne and Wear") [metropolitan county](/wiki/Metropolitan_county "Metropolitan county") in 1974.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The [county council](/wiki/County_council "County council") has been governed from [Morpeth](/wiki/Morpeth,_Northumberland "Morpeth, Northumberland") since 1981. In 2009 the county council became a [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority") when the county's districts were abolished and the county council took on their functions.

### Council

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Council")]

Main article: [Northumberland Council](/wiki/Northumberland_Council "Northumberland Council")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/County_Hall%2C_Morpeth_%28geograph_4464710%29.jpg/250px-County_Hall%2C_Morpeth_%28geograph_4464710%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:County_Hall,_Morpeth_(geograph_4464710).jpg)

[County Hall](/wiki/County_Hall,_Morpeth "County Hall, Morpeth"), Morpeth

The [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority") of [Northumberland Council](/wiki/Northumberland_Council "Northumberland Council") was [established in April 2009](/wiki/2009_structural_changes_to_local_government_in_England "2009 structural changes to local government in England"). Previously, the county had a two-tier council system for the [county](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county") and its six [districts](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_district "Non-metropolitan district"), both responsible for different aspects of [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_the_United_Kingdom "Local government in the United Kingdom"). These districts were [Blyth Valley](/wiki/Blyth_Valley "Blyth Valley"), [Wansbeck](/wiki/Wansbeck_District "Wansbeck District"), [Castle Morpeth](/wiki/Castle_Morpeth "Castle Morpeth"), [Tynedale](/wiki/Tynedale "Tynedale"), [Alnwick](/wiki/Alnwick_(district) "Alnwick (district)") and [Berwick-upon-Tweed](/wiki/Berwick-upon-Tweed_(borough) "Berwick-upon-Tweed (borough)").

On 23 June 2016 Northumberland took part in the UK-wide referendum on the UK's membership of the EU. In Northumberland a majority voted to leave the European Union. The only one of the four parliamentary constituencies in Northumberland to vote Remain was [Hexham](/wiki/Hexham_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Hexham (UK Parliament constituency)").

## Demography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Demography")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(September 2018)* |

At the [2001 UK Census](/wiki/United_Kingdom_census,_2001 "United Kingdom census, 2001") Northumberland registered a population of 307,190,[[20]](#cite_note-2001_population-20) estimated to be 309,237 in 2003,[[21]](#cite_note-2003_population-21) The 2011 UK Census gave a population of 316,028.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In 2001 there were 130,780 households, 10% of the population were retired, and one-third rented their homes. Northumberland has an ethnic minority population at 0.985% of the population, far lower compared to the average of 9.1% for England as a whole. In the [2001 UK Census](/wiki/United_Kingdom_census,_2001 "United Kingdom census, 2001"), 81% of the population reported their religion as Christianity, 0.8% as "other religion", and 12% as having no religion.[[23]](#cite_note-2001_religion-23)

Being primarily rural with significant areas of upland, the population density of Northumberland is only 66 persons per square kilometre, giving it one of the [lowest population density in England](/wiki/List_of_English_districts_by_population_density "List of English districts by population density").

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Housedon_hill.jpg/250px-Housedon_hill.jpg)](/wiki/File:Housedon_hill.jpg)

[Housedon Hill](/wiki/Housedon_Hill "Housedon Hill")

Northumberland's industry is dominated by some multinational corporations: [Coca-Cola](/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola"), MSD, [GE](/wiki/GE "GE") and [Drager](/wiki/Dr%C3%A4gerwerk "Drägerwerk") all have significant facilities in the region.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Tourism is a major source of employment and income in Northumberland. In the early 2000s the county annually received 1.1 million British visitors and 50,000 foreign tourists, who spent a total of £162 million.

[Coal mining](/wiki/History_of_coal_mining "History of coal mining") in the county goes back to [Tudor times](/wiki/Tudor_period "Tudor period"). Coal mines continue to operate today; many of them are open-cast mines. Planning approval was given in January 2014 for an open-cast mine at [Halton Lea Gate](/wiki/Halton_Lea_Gate "Halton Lea Gate") near [Lambley](/wiki/Lambley,_Northumberland "Lambley, Northumberland").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

A major employer in Northumberland is [Hexham](/wiki/Hexham "Hexham")-based [Egger (UK) Limited](/wiki/Egger_(company) "Egger (company)").[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Pharmaceuticals, health care and biotechnology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Pharmaceuticals, health care and biotechnology")]

[Pharmaceutical](/wiki/Pharmaceutical "Pharmaceutical"), health care and emerging [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology") companies form a very significant part of the county's economy.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Many of these companies are part of the approximately 11,000-worker[[29]](#cite_note-29) [Northeast of England Process Industry Cluster](/wiki/Northeast_of_England_Process_Industry_Cluster "Northeast of England Process Industry Cluster") (NEPIC) and include Aesica Pharmaceuticals,[[30]](#cite_note-30) Arcinova, [MSD](/wiki/Merck_Sharp_and_Dohme "Merck Sharp and Dohme"), [Piramal Healthcare](/wiki/Piramal_Healthcare "Piramal Healthcare"), [Procter & Gamble](/wiki/Procter_%26_Gamble "Procter & Gamble"), [Shire Plc](/wiki/Shire_(pharmaceutical_company) "Shire (pharmaceutical company)") (formerly SCM Pharma),[[31]](#cite_note-31) Shasun Pharma Solutions,[[32]](#cite_note-32) Specials Laboratory,[[33]](#cite_note-33) and [Thermo Fisher Scientific](/wiki/Thermo_Fisher_Scientific "Thermo Fisher Scientific"). The cluster also includes Cambridge Bioresearch, GlaxoSmithKline, Fujifilm Diosynth Biotech, Leica Bio, Data Trial, High Force Research, Non-Linear Dynamics, and Immuno Diagnostic Systems (IDS). The towns of Alnwick, Cramlington, Morpeth, Prudhoe all have significant pharmaceutical factories and laboratories.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

[Newcastle University](/wiki/Newcastle_University "Newcastle University") and [Northumbria University](/wiki/Northumbria_University "Northumbria University") are the leading academic institutions nearby. The local industry includes commercial or academic activity in pre-clinical research and development, clinical research and development, pilot-scale manufacturing, full-scale active pharmaceutical ingredient/intermediate manufacturing, formulation, packaging, and distribution.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Media")]

Having no large population centres, the county's mainstream media outlets are served from nearby [Tyne and Wear](/wiki/Tyne_and_Wear "Tyne and Wear"), including radio stations and [television channels](/wiki/Television_channel "Television channel") (such as [BBC Look North](/wiki/BBC_Look_North_(North_East_and_Cumbria) "BBC Look North (North East and Cumbria)"), [BBC Radio Newcastle](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Newcastle "BBC Radio Newcastle"), [ITV Tyne Tees](/wiki/ITV_Tyne_Tees "ITV Tyne Tees") and [Hits Radio North East](/wiki/Hits_Radio_North_East "Hits Radio North East")), along with the majority of daily newspapers covering the area (*[The Journal](/wiki/The_Journal_(Newcastle_upon_Tyne_newspaper) "The Journal (Newcastle upon Tyne newspaper)")*, *[Evening Chronicle](/wiki/Evening_Chronicle "Evening Chronicle")*). It is worth remembering however that although Northumberland, like many administrative areas in England, has been shorn of its geographical regional centre, that centre—Newcastle upon Tyne—remains an essential element within the entity we know as Northumberland. Newcastle's newspapers are as widely read in its Northumbrian hinterland as any of those of the wider county: the *[Northumberland Gazette](/wiki/Northumberland_Gazette "Northumberland Gazette")*, *[Morpeth Herald](/wiki/Morpeth_Herald "Morpeth Herald")*, *[Berwick Advertiser](/w/index.php?title=Berwick_Advertiser&action=edit&redlink=1 "Berwick Advertiser (page does not exist)")*, *[Hexham Courant](/wiki/Hexham_Courant "Hexham Courant")* and the *[News Post Leader](/wiki/News_Post_Leader "News Post Leader")*.

[Lionheart Radio](/wiki/Lionheart_Radio "Lionheart Radio"), a [community radio](/wiki/Community_radio "Community radio") station based in [Alnwick](/wiki/Alnwick "Alnwick"), has been awarded a community broadcasting licence by [Ofcom](/wiki/Ofcom "Ofcom").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Businesses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Businesses")]

Ashington has the former [Alcan Lynemouth Aluminium Smelter](/wiki/Alcan_Lynemouth_Aluminium_Smelter "Alcan Lynemouth Aluminium Smelter"), next to the [Lynemouth Power Station](/wiki/Lynemouth_Power_Station "Lynemouth Power Station"). [Hammerite](/wiki/Hammerite "Hammerite") and Cuprinol are made in [Prudhoe](/wiki/Prudhoe "Prudhoe") by [ICI Paints](/wiki/Imperial_Chemical_Industries "Imperial Chemical Industries"). A [Procter & Gamble](/wiki/Procter_%26_Gamble "Procter & Gamble") factory in [Seaton Delaval](/wiki/Seaton_Delaval "Seaton Delaval") makes [Hugo Boss](/wiki/Hugo_Boss "Hugo Boss") aftershave and [Clairol](/wiki/Clairol "Clairol") and [Nice 'n Easy](/wiki/Nice_%27n_Easy_(hair_coloring) "Nice 'n Easy (hair coloring)") hair dye at a site formerly owned by Shultons, who originated [Old Spice](/wiki/Old_Spice "Old Spice") and were bought by P&G in 1990. [McQuay](/wiki/McQuay_International "McQuay International") UK makes air conditioning systems on the Bassington Industrial Estate at the A1068/A1172 junction in [Cramlington](/wiki/Cramlington "Cramlington"), and [Avery Dennison](/wiki/Avery_Dennison "Avery Dennison") UK make labels on the Nelson Industrial Estate off of the A192. Schweppes' [Abbey Well](/wiki/Abbey_Well "Abbey Well") mineral water is made by [Coca-Cola](/wiki/Coca-Cola_Enterprises "Coca-Cola Enterprises") in the east of [Morpeth](/wiki/Morpeth,_Northumberland "Morpeth, Northumberland"). The [National Renewable Energy Centre](/wiki/National_Renewable_Energy_Centre_(Narec) "National Renewable Energy Centre (Narec)") (Narec) is at Blyth.

### Football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Football")]

A [precursor of modern football](/wiki/Scoring_the_Hales "Scoring the Hales") is still seen in the region at some annual [Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday") games at [Alnwick](/wiki/Alnwick "Alnwick").[[59]](#cite_note-59) In 1280 at [Ulgham](/wiki/Ulgham "Ulgham") near [Morpeth](/wiki/Morpeth,_Northumberland "Morpeth, Northumberland") Northumberland, records show that Henry of Ellington was killed playing football when David Le Keu's knife went into Henry's belly and killed him.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-Magoun-61) Organised football teams as we know today did not appear until the 1870s. [Newcastle United Football Club](/wiki/Newcastle_United_Football_Club "Newcastle United Football Club") was formed in 1892 by uniting [Newcastle West End FC](/wiki/Newcastle_West_End_F.C. "Newcastle West End F.C.") with [Newcastle East End](/wiki/Newcastle_East_End_F.C. "Newcastle East End F.C.").[[62]](#cite_note-Hutchinson_1997-62)

Newcastle United were first division champions three times in the early 20th century, reaching the FA Cup Final three times before winning it at the fourth attempt in 1910.[[63]](#cite_note-63)
Today, top quality professional football remains in Northumberland. Since the 2017 – 18 season, Newcastle United is a [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") team. [St James' Park](/wiki/St_James%27_Park "St James' Park") in Newcastle is a first class football venue, often used for international games at all levels. [Blyth Spartans A.F.C.](/wiki/Blyth_Spartans_A.F.C. "Blyth Spartans A.F.C.") have had success and public attention through [Football Association Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") runs.

#### Notable associated footballers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Notable associated footballers")]

There are many notable footballers from the county, pre Second World War and immediate post war greats were [George Camsell](/wiki/George_Camsell "George Camsell") and [Hughie Gallacher](/wiki/Hughie_Gallacher "Hughie Gallacher"), these were described in the "Clown Prince of Football" by [Len Shackleton](/wiki/Len_Shackleton "Len Shackleton"). The author played for Newcastle United and [Northumberland County Cricket Club](/wiki/Northumberland_County_Cricket_Club "Northumberland County Cricket Club"). Shackleton's book was controversial when it was first published because chapter 9, named "The Average Director's Knowledge of Football", was produced as a blank page.[[64]](#cite_note-64) Notable players after the Second World War included [Joe Harvey](/wiki/Joe_Harvey_(footballer) "Joe Harvey (footballer)"), [Jackie Milburn](/wiki/Jackie_Milburn "Jackie Milburn"),[[65]](#cite_note-65) [Brian Clough](/wiki/Brian_Clough "Brian Clough")[[66]](#cite_note-66) and Newcastle's [Bobby Moncur](/wiki/Bobby_Moncur "Bobby Moncur") who led his team to win the Inter City Fairs Cup in 1969.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

Two of Jackie Milburn's nephews from [Ashington](/wiki/Ashington "Ashington"), [Bobby Charlton](/wiki/Bobby_Charlton "Bobby Charlton") and [Jackie Charlton](/wiki/Jackie_Charlton "Jackie Charlton") are perhaps the two most significant players for [England](/wiki/England_National_Football_Team "England National Football Team").[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) Bobby joined [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") and Jackie [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United "Leeds United") both contributing much to the success and history of their respective clubs. They both became permanent fixtures in [Alf Ramsey's](/wiki/Alf_Ramsey "Alf Ramsey") 1966 England World Cup winning team.[[70]](#cite_note-70) [Malcolm Macdonald](/wiki/Malcolm_Macdonald "Malcolm Macdonald") was a successful Newcastle player of the 1970s. Great national players who played at Northumberland clubs in the 1980s and 1990s include [Peter Beardsley](/wiki/Peter_Beardsley "Peter Beardsley"), [Paul Gascoigne](/wiki/Paul_Gascoigne "Paul Gascoigne"), [Chris Waddle](/wiki/Chris_Waddle "Chris Waddle") and [Alan Shearer](/wiki/Alan_Shearer "Alan Shearer"). Shearer remains the highest scoring player in Premier League history with 260 goals in 441 appearances.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### Horseracing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Horseracing")]

Early races were held at Newcastle's Killingworth Moor from 1632 before moving to the Town Moor. The 'Pitmen's Derby' or [Northumberland Plate](/wiki/Northumberland_Plate "Northumberland Plate") was held from 1833 and moved to [Gosforth](/wiki/Gosforth "Gosforth") in 1882.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Modern day horse racing still takes place at [Newcastle Racecourse](/wiki/Newcastle_Racecourse "Newcastle Racecourse").[[73]](#cite_note-73)

### Golf

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Golf")]

Golf is a Scottish import to many countries, but it is said to have been played in this region by [St Cuthbert](/wiki/St_Cuthbert "St Cuthbert") on the dunes of the Northumberland coast. The oldest club in Northumberland was at [Alnmouth](/wiki/Alnmouth "Alnmouth"), founded in 1869, it is the fourth oldest in the country and is now Alnmouth Village Club; it is a 9 hole links course.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

There is one old [links](/wiki/Links_(golf) "Links (golf)") course at [Goswick](/wiki/Goswick "Goswick"). It is a James Braid design which is widely acknowledged as a classic Northumberland links course;[[75]](#cite_note-75) so much so, that the Royal and Ancient Golf Club (R&A) chose Goswick as a regional qualifier for the [Open Championship](/wiki/Open_Championship "Open Championship") for five years from 2008.

During the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") of 1642–1651, [King Charles](/wiki/King_Charles_I_of_England "King Charles I of England") played 'Goff' in the Shield Fields suburb of Pandon during his imprisonment in the town.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

Today inland golf courses are abundant in the county,[[77]](#cite_note-77)

The county has a professional golfer who has played in many professional golf tour events: [Kenny Ferrie](/wiki/Kenneth_Ferrie "Kenneth Ferrie") from [Ashington](/wiki/Ashington "Ashington"), who has won events on the prestigious [European Tour](/wiki/European_Tour "European Tour").

Main article: [List of schools in Northumberland](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Northumberland "List of schools in Northumberland")

Northumberland has a completely [comprehensive](/wiki/Comprehensive_School "Comprehensive School") education system, with 15 [state schools](/wiki/State_schools "State schools"), two academies and one independent school. Like [Bedfordshire](/wiki/Bedfordshire "Bedfordshire"), it embraced the comprehensive ideal with the [three-tier](/wiki/Three-tier_education "Three-tier education") system of lower/middle/upper schools with large school year sizes (often around 300). This eliminated choice of school in most areas: instead of having two secondary schools in one town, one school became a middle school and another became an upper school. A programme introduced in 2006 known as Putting the Learner First has eliminated this structure in the former areas of Blyth Valley and Wansbeck, where two-tier education has been introduced. Although the two processes are not officially connected, the introduction of two tiers has coincided with the move to build academy schools in Blyth, with [Bede Academy](/wiki/Bede_Academy "Bede Academy") and in Ashington at Hirst. One response to these changes has been the decision of [Ponteland High School](/wiki/Ponteland_High_School "Ponteland High School") to apply for Trust status.

[Cramlington Learning Village](/wiki/Cramlington_Learning_Village "Cramlington Learning Village") has almost 400 pupils in each school year, making it one of the largest schools in England. [The Blyth Academy](/wiki/The_Blyth_Academy "The Blyth Academy") in southeast Northumberland can hold 1,500 students throughout the building. [Astley Community High School](/wiki/Astley_Community_High_School "Astley Community High School") in [Seaton Delaval](/wiki/Seaton_Delaval "Seaton Delaval"), which accepts students from Seaton Delaval, [Seaton Sluice](/wiki/Seaton_Sluice "Seaton Sluice") and [Blyth](/wiki/Blyth,_Northumberland "Blyth, Northumberland"), has been the subject of controversial remarks from politicians claiming it would no longer be viable once Bede Academy opened in Blyth, a claim strongly disputed by the headteacher.

The county of Northumberland is served by one Catholic high school, [St Benet Biscop Catholic Academy](/wiki/St_Benet_Biscop_Catholic_Academy "St Benet Biscop Catholic Academy") in [Bedlington](/wiki/Bedlington "Bedlington"), which is attended by students from all over the area. Students from Northumberland also attend independent schools such as the [Royal Grammar School](/wiki/Royal_Grammar_School,_Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Royal Grammar School, Newcastle upon Tyne") in Newcastle.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/2012-07-03_Bloody_Crane%27s-bill%2C_Hauxley%2C_Northumberland_4.jpg/250px-2012-07-03_Bloody_Crane%27s-bill%2C_Hauxley%2C_Northumberland_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:2012-07-03_Bloody_Crane%27s-bill,_Hauxley,_Northumberland_4.jpg)

Bloody crane's-bill at Hauxley on the Northumberland coast

Northumberland has traditions not found elsewhere in England. These include the [rapper sword](/wiki/Rapper_sword "Rapper sword") dance, the [clog dance](/wiki/Clog_dancing#English_clog_dancing "Clog dancing") and the [Northumbrian smallpipe](/wiki/Northumbrian_smallpipe "Northumbrian smallpipe"). Northumberland also has its own [tartan or check](/wiki/Northumbrian_tartan "Northumbrian tartan"), sometimes referred to in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") as the Shepherd's Tartan. Traditional [Northumbrian music](/wiki/Music_of_Northumbria "Music of Northumbria") has more similarity to [Lowland Scottish](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") and Irish music than it does to that of other parts of England, reflecting the strong historical links between Northumbria and the [Lowlands](/wiki/Scottish_Lowlands "Scottish Lowlands") of [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), and the large Irish population on Tyneside.

The [border ballads](/wiki/Border_ballads "Border ballads") of the region have been famous since late mediaeval times. [Thomas Percy](/wiki/Thomas_Percy_(Bishop_of_Dromore) "Thomas Percy (Bishop of Dromore)"), whose celebrated *[Reliques of Ancient English Poetry](/wiki/Reliques_of_Ancient_English_Poetry "Reliques of Ancient English Poetry")* appeared in 1765, states that most of the minstrels who sang the border ballads in London and elsewhere in the 15th and 16th centuries belonged to the North. The activities of [Sir Walter Scott](/wiki/Sir_Walter_Scott "Sir Walter Scott") and others in the 19th century gave the ballads an even wider popularity. [William Morris](/wiki/William_Morris "William Morris") considered them to be the greatest poems in the language, while [Algernon Charles Swinburne](/wiki/Algernon_Charles_Swinburne "Algernon Charles Swinburne") knew virtually all of them by heart.

One of the best-known is the stirring ["Chevy Chase"](/wiki/The_Ballad_of_Chevy_Chase "The Ballad of Chevy Chase"), which tells of the Earl of Northumberland's vow to hunt for three days across the Border "maugre the doughty Douglas". Of it, the Elizabethan courtier, soldier and poet [Sir Philip Sidney](/wiki/Sir_Philip_Sidney "Sir Philip Sidney") famously said, "I never heard the old song of Percy and Douglas that I found not my heart moved more than with a trumpet". [Ben Jonson](/wiki/Ben_Jonson "Ben Jonson") said that he would give all his works to have written "Chevy Chase".

Overall the culture of Northumberland, as with the [North East](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England") of England in general, may have more in common with Scottish Lowland culture than with that of Southern England. Both regions have their cultural origins in the old [Anglian](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)") [Kingdom of Northumbria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Northumbria "Kingdom of Northumbria"), a fact borne out by the linguistic links between the two regions. These include many [Old English](/wiki/Old_English_language "Old English language") words not found in other forms of [Modern English](/wiki/Modern_English "Modern English"), such as *bairn* for child (see [Scots language](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language") and [Northumbrian dialect](/wiki/Northumbrian_dialect "Northumbrian dialect")).[[78]](#cite_note-northeastengland.talktalk.net-78)[[79]](#cite_note-northumbriana.org.uk-79)

The lands just north or south of the border have long shared certain aspects of history and heritage; it is thus thought by some that the [Anglo-Scottish border](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_border "Anglo-Scottish border") is largely political rather than cultural.[[79]](#cite_note-northumbriana.org.uk-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80)

Attempts to raise the level of awareness of Northumberland culture have also started, with the formation of a Northumbrian Language Society to preserve the unique dialects ([Pitmatic](/wiki/Pitmatic "Pitmatic") and other [Northumbrian](/wiki/English_of_Northumbria "English of Northumbria") dialects) of this region, as well as to promote home-grown talent.[[78]](#cite_note-northeastengland.talktalk.net-78)[[79]](#cite_note-northumbriana.org.uk-79)

Northumberland's [county flower](/wiki/County_flower "County flower") is the bloody crane's-bill (*[Geranium sanguineum](/wiki/Geranium_sanguineum "Geranium sanguineum")*) and its affiliated Royal Navy ship is its namesake, [HMS *Northumberland*](/wiki/HMS_Northumberland_(F238) "HMS Northumberland (F238)").

### Flag

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northumberland&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Flag")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Flag_of_Northumberland.svg/250px-Flag_of_Northumberland.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Northumberland.svg)

The flag of the historic county of Northumberland

The historic county of Northumberland has [its own flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Northumberland "Flag of Northumberland"), which is a banner of the arms of Northumberland County Council. The shield of arms is in turn based on the arms medieval heralds had attributed to the [Kingdom of Bernicia](/wiki/Bernicia "Bernicia") (which the first County Council used until it was granted its own arms). The Bernician arms were fictional but inspired by [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede")'s brief description of a flag used on the tomb of [St Oswald](/wiki/Oswald_of_Northumbria "Oswald of Northumbria") in the 7th century.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

The current arms were granted to the county council in 1951, and adopted as the flag of Northumberland in 1995.[[82]](#cite_note-flag-82)
